current_w: The Oscar Wilde Memorial Sculpture is a collection of three statues in Merrion Square in Dublin, Ireland, commemorating Irish poet and playwright Oscar Wilde. The sculptures were unveiled in 1997 and were designed and made by Danny Osborne.
hook: A reclining Wilde carved from semi-precious stones gathered across three continents, lounging on a quartz boulder opposite the house where he grew up
facts: Created by English sculptor Danny Osborne; commissioned by the Guinness Ireland Group and unveiled in 1997 by Wilde's grandson Merlin Holland

The figure is built from coloured stones: a green nephrite jade smoking jacket, pink Norwegian thulite collar and cuffs, white jade head and hands, Blue Pearl granite trousers and black Indian granite shoes
Wilde reclines on a large quartz boulder brought from the Wicklow Mountains, in Merrion Square gardens diagonally opposite his childhood home at No. 1 Merrion Square
The face is deliberately two-sided — smiling on one profile, sombre on the other — meant to capture Wilde's wit alongside the suffering of his imprisonment
Two flanking pillars are inscribed with quotations from Wilde, the handwriting copied from figures including Seamus Heaney, John B. Keane and Michael D. Higgins
The memorial is a group of three: Wilde himself, plus a bronze of his pregnant wife Constance Lloyd and a male torso of Dionysus
legend: Dubliners have nicknamed the colourful reclining figure 'The Fag on the Crag' and 'The Quare on the Square'

sources.note: Materials, designer, 1997 unveiling, three statues, inscribed pillars, quartz boulder
Sculptor's own page on the monument and stone choices

notes: Nickname phrasing varies across sources; framed as legend, not stated as fact.

current_w: Only surviving gate in the Dublin city walls, Ireland. Built in 1240.
hook: The last of Dublin's 19 medieval city gates still standing — a 13th-century arch you can still walk through behind St Audoen's Church
facts: It is the sole surviving city gate of medieval Dublin, inserted through the earlier Hiberno-Norse wall in the 13th century
At its greatest extent the Anglo-Norman walled city had as many as 19 gates, almost all since demolished as Dublin expanded
The gate takes its name from the adjacent St Audoen's Church (Audoen = St Ouen of Rouen) on High Street
Only two stretches of the old city wall survive visibly above ground — at St Audoen's and nearby Cornmarket
Medieval Dublin's gates doubled as toll points where taxes were collected on goods entering the city
The gateway was restored in 1976 and is still used as a pedestrian route up to High Street and Cornmarket

sources.note: 13th-century date, only surviving gate, surviving wall stretches, St Audoen's location
Tourism overview confirming sole surviving gate and toll-booth function

notes: The '1240' build date in the current writeup is plausible (13th century) but the precise year is not firmly corroborated by these sources, which give '13th century'; kept as broad date in facts.

current_w: Public statue in Dublin. Work by Paul Daly. Built in 2005.
hook: A life-size bronze of Thin Lizzy's frontman, guitar in hand, standing outside Bruxelles bar on Harry Street where he used to drink
facts: Bronze statue of Thin Lizzy frontman Phil Lynott, unveiled 19 August 2005 outside Bruxelles pub on Harry Street, off Grafton Street
Sculpted by Paul Daly and cast by Leo Higgins at the Cast foundry in The Liberties
Unveiled by Lynott's mother Philomena, the result of years of campaigning by her and the Roisin Dubh Trust, in a joint project with Dublin City Council
The unveiling was attended by former Thin Lizzy bandmates Gary Moore, Brian Robertson and Scott Gorham
Lynott is depicted in a characteristic stage pose holding his bass guitar

sources.note: Unveiling date, sculptor, foundry, attendees, location
Dublin.ie feature on the statue and its Harry Street location
